project_title_fr: Ciblage du fret aérien préalable au chargement (CFAPC)
branch: Passenger Protect Program and Targeting Operations
description_en:
PACT aims to identify and apply risk mitigation measures to inbound high-risk air shipments that could contain concealed improvised explosive devices or other threat items prior to loading and departure to Canada. This is achieved through the risk assessment of advanced cargo information from air carriers with the assistance of emerging technologies. After exhausting manual methods for data analysis, the PACT pilot was selected by the Deputy Minister’s Committee on Policy Innovation in 2018 as a test case to determine if the use of advanced analytics powered by artificial intelligence (AI) could help manage the workload. During the 2018-2019 fiscal year, a proof of concept was carried out which demonstrated that these emerging technologies could indeed be applied to the categorization and risk assessment of advanced cargo information (i.e. shipper and consignee name and address, cargo weight, description and piece count, and air waybill number). The PACT system accepts industry standard messaging in the form of digital air waybills which contain all eight required data elements. With respect to interpreting free text fields in airwaybills – a data field that is rife with typos and industry jargon – the ability of the technology to categorize the results effectively and efficiently within a risk assessment framework made the expansion of PACT possible.

The PACT system will sort and categorize the data and apply a risk algorithm to flag potentially high-risk shipments to the PACT analyst. The shipments whose characteristics fall within a pre-defined low risk scenario are auto-assessed and are not presented to a PACT analyst for review. Conversely, the shipments that fall outside these parameters are presented to PACT analysts, who then make a decision to apply a risk mitigation measure to an air cargo shipment prior to it being loaded on an aircraft destined for Canada. These take the forms of Requests for Information (RFI), Requests for Screening (RFS) or Do Not Load (DNL) notices.
description_fr:
Le ciblage du fret aérien préalable au chargement (CFAPC) vise à déceler les envois de fret aérien à haut risque, qui pourraient contenir des engins explosifs improvisés dissimulés ou d’autres éléments de menace, avant le chargement du fret et le départ vers le Canada, et à leur appliquer des mesures d’atténuation des risques. Pour ce faire, on procède à l’évaluation des risques à l’aide de technologies émergentes, au moyen de l’information préalable fournie par les transporteurs aériens. Après avoir épuisé les méthodes manuelles d’analyse des données, le projet pilote de CFAPC a été sélectionné par le Comité des sous-ministres sur l’innovation en matière de politiques en 2018 pour servir d’essai afin d’établir si l’utilisation d’analyses avancées alimentées par l’intelligence artificielle (IA) pourrait aider à gérer la charge de travail. Au cours de l’exercice 2018-2019, une validation de principe a été réalisée, qui a démontré que ces technologies émergentes pouvaient effectivement être appliquées à la catégorisation et à l’évaluation des risques de l’information préalable sur le fret. Cette information comprend le nom et l’adresse de l’expéditeur et du destinataire, le poids, la description et le nombre d’articles de fret, ainsi que le numéro de la lettre de transport aérien. Le système de CFAPC accepte les messages standard du secteur sous la forme de lettres de transport aérien numériques qui contiennent tous les huit éléments de données requis. Concernant l’interprétation des champs de texte libre des lettres de transport aérien — un champ de données truffé de fautes de frappe et de jargon du secteur — la capacité de la technologie à catégoriser les résultats de manière efficace et efficiente dans un cadre d’évaluation des risques a rendu possible la croissance du CFAPC.

Le système de CFAPC triera et classera les données et appliquera un algorithme de risque pour signaler à l’analyste de CFAPC les envois pouvant présenter un risque potentiellement élevé. Les envois dont les caractéristiques relèvent d’un scénario prédéfini de faible risque sont évalués automatiquement et ne sont pas soumis à l’examen d’un analyste de CFAPC. Inversement, les envois qui ne correspondent pas à ces paramètres sont présentés aux analystes de CFAPC, qui peuvent alors décider d'appliquer une mesure d’atténuation des risques à un envoi de fret aérien avant qu’il ne soit chargé à bord d’un avion à destination du Canada. Ces demandes prennent la forme de demandes de renseignements (RFI), de demandes de contrôle (RFS) ou d’avis « ne pas charger » (DNL).

After exhausting manual methods for data analysis, the PACT pilot was selected by the Deputy Minister’s Committee on Policy Innovation in 2018 as a test case to determine if the use of advanced analytics powered by artificial intelligence (AI) could help manage the workload. During the 2018-2019 fiscal year, a proof of concept was carried out which demonstrated that these emerging technologies could indeed be applied to the categorization and risk assessment of advanced cargo information (i.e. shipper and consignee name and address, cargo weight, description and piece count, and air waybill number). The PACT system accepts industry standard messaging in the form of digital air waybills which contain all eight required data elements. The shipments whose characteristics fall within a pre-defined low risk scenario are auto-assessed and are not presented to a PACT analyst for review. These take the forms of Requests for Information (RFI), Requests for Screening (RFS) or Do Not Load (DNL) notices.", "projectDetailsProgram": "Pre-load Air Cargo Targeting (PACT) Program", "businessDrivers1": ["item2", "item3", "item4", "item5"], "riskProfile1": "item2-0", "riskProfile2": "item2-0", "riskProfile3": "item1-4", "riskProfile4": "item2-0", "projectAuthority1": "item2-0", "aboutSystem1": ["item2", "item3", "item4", "item5"], "aboutAlgorithm1": "item2-0", "aboutAlgorithm2": "item2-0", "decisionSector1": ["item6-1"], "decisionSector2": "Aviation security - air cargo security", "impact1": "item1-1", "impact2": "item2-0", "impact3": "item1-4", "impact5": "item2-0", "impact6": "item2-2", "impact7": "item1-1", "impact8": "When a request for screening is made, the hope is that the screening reveals there is no concealed improvised explosive device or other threat item, and the shipment can proceed. In the worst-case scenario (where the shipment is prevented from being loaded because of an unresolved flag), the impact is isolated to the singular shipment (e.g., to one box or one pallet) and would typically follow an unsuccessful screening attempt (i.e., where the screening cannot rule out the presence of a threat item). In these cases, emergency response teams on-site will take over and the issue is no longer under PACT's purview. 

The impact of the decision not to load a shipment is limited to the flight for which the shipment is booked and would only apply in circumstances where the threat of an explosive device cannot be ruled out. Should information become available that would clear the flag after the flight has departed, the cargo would be treated as a new shipment in the system and would be re-integrated into the supply chain to proceed for transport. The decision to issue a DNL notice has a very low likelihood of occurrence. As such, the likelihood of DNL-related delays is also limited. Air carriers’ ability to provide the PLACI data to PACT as early as practicable prior to the cargo being loaded onto the aircraft will also help to mitigate potential delays. In this way, air carriers would help ensure that they are fulfilling their terms of service for cargo carriage, and that the impacts from any risk mitigation decisions by PACT remain brief.

Ultimately, decisions to apply a risk mitigation measure to a shipment are made by PACT analysts in a small number of cases and can take a matter of seconds to be cleared/reversed. For example, a shipment may be flagged for review by the system and then cleared by an analyst within seconds without the need for additional information. The change in status is communicated to the air carrier in the system immediately following the decision. In the case where additional information is requested and received, the decision to prevent the
shipment from being loaded is reversed as soon as possible.", "impact9": "item1-1", "impact10": "Shippers who opt to ship and receive cargo through the air cargo supply chain (i.e., shipping on passenger or all-cargo flights), have and retain the right to choose this means from among alternative modes to ship cargo (e.g., by airmail or the marine or land modes (e.g., rail, road)). When a shipper opts to ship via air cargo, there are terms and conditions that apply to this means of shipment, and the contract into which they enter with the air carrier stipulates (pursuant to various conventions) which information is being collected and shared and that the shipment will be subject to all applicable laws that govern the shipment of air cargo. Should a shipment be
denied loading, the shipper can invoke their rights to seek remedies under their contract of carriage or those available under international treaties that govern liability regimes for the shipment of goods on board aircraft. In the case where a PACT decision prevents their cargo from being loaded, they will also be provided contact information for the Program through which they can request an explanation.", "impact11": "item1-1", "impact13": "item1-1", "impact15": "item1-1", "aboutDataSource1": "item1-4", "aboutDataSource9": "item1-0", "aboutDataSource10": "item1-2", "aboutDataSource11": "item1-0", "aboutDataSource2": "item2-1", "aboutDataSource3": "item4-3", "aboutDataSource4": "item1-4", "aboutDataSource5": "item2-0", "aboutDataSource6": "item1-4", "aboutDataSource7": "item1-1", "aboutDataSource8": "item4-4", "aboutDataType1": "item1", "aboutDataType2": ["item1-2"], "consultationImplementation1": "item1-1", "consultationImplementation2": ["item3", "item2", "item5", "item6", "item7", "item8", "item4", "item9", "item13"], "consultationImplementation3": "item1-1", "consultationImplementation4": ["item8", "item9"], "consultationImplementation4-other": "AeroMexico

", "dataQualityImplementation1": "item1-2", "dataQualityImplementation2": "item2-0", "dataQualityImplementation3": "item2-0", "dataQualityImplementation4": "item2-0", "dataQualityImplementation5": "item1-1", "dataQualityImplementation6": "item2-0", "dataQualityImplementation7": "item1-2", "dataQualityImplementation8": "item1-2", "dataQualityImplementation9": "item2-0", "dataQualityImplementation10": "item2-0", "fairnessImplementation1": "item2-0", "fairnessImplementation2": "item1-2", "fairnessImplementation3": "item1-2", "fairnessImplementation4": "item1-1", "fairnessImplementation5": "item1-2", "fairnessImplementation6": "item1-1", "fairnessImplementation7": "item1-1", "fairnessImplementation8": "item1-2", "fairnessImplementation9": "item1-1", "fairnessImplementation10": "item1-2", "fairnessImplementation11": "item1-1", "fairnessImplementation12": "item1-1", "fairnessImplementation13": "item1-2", "fairnessImplementation14": "item1-2", "fairnessImplementation15": "item1-1", "fairnessImplementation16": "item1-2", "fairnessImplementation17": "item1-1", "privacyImplementation1": "item1-1", "privacyImplementation2": "item1-1", "privacyImplementation3": "item2-0", "privacyImplementation4": "item1-1", "impact12": "The premise of the PACT Program (and others like it around the world) is to ensure the continued flow of goods around the globe while adding a layer of security to protect the supply chain from unlawful acts of interference. Using data to perform a risk assessment on a shipment sometimes weeks in advance of loading is widely accepted (see the International Civil Aviation Organization and World Customs Organization's Joint Guiding Principles on Pre-Loading Advanced Cargo Information) to be an efficient and effective means to address potential risk in the least obtrusive means available. In the case of life-saving medical supplies, the supply chain is carefully planned out and follows predictable routes involving known players. Such shipments are also clearly labelled and often have specific handling codes that protect passage from one entity to the next.  This allows for a simplified risk assessment, lessens the chance of shipments being delayed in any way, and in fact facilitates the safe movement of life-saving materials. If life-saving materials were to be prevented from being transported on a specific flight, it would be as a result of a threat item being identified, which if unmitigated, would endanger lives. ", "impact16": "The PACT Program, including its system design and related decision-making processes was assessed in accordance with the Cabinet Directive on the Environmental Assessment of Policy, Plan and Program Proposals. The Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) process was followed and a Sustainable Transportation Assessment (STA) was completed by Transport Canada's Environmental Management Branch. The assessment found that the PACT Program is not expected to result in important environmental effects, positive or negative.", "consultationImplementation2-other": "CBSA", "businessDrivers4": "By enabling the implementation of the PACT program, the system will benefit the travelling public and air industry workers by adding a layer of security to the air cargo supply chain, which includes both passenger and all-cargo flights.", "businessDrivers3": "Overall, industry is looking for a seamless and digital means of sending advanced cargo information and receiving status updates and referrals in a timely manner. In addition to providing a secure means of shipping, air carriers are committed to the provision of an expedient service to their clients (including individuals and businesses seeking to ship cargo to Canada). 

Air carriers and data aggregators have been directly involved in the system development to establish a data format and means of transmission that are aligned with industry standards. Extensive industry consultation has been undertaken during the PACT pilots since 2012 and the views that were recorded have long informed the program design (including the system parameters). Feedback has stressed the importance of expediency in risk assessments to minimize operational impacts on shippers and other supply chain partners.   

Primary users of the system (Transport Canada employees) have been involved in its design and testing to ensure it is user-friendly and meets the business need: a system that can receive, sort, risk and display results from a large volume of data from various sources through a web API.  ", "businessDrivers5": "item3-0", "businessDrivers6": "The benefit of the automated system is the expediting of a risk assessment process that adds a layer of security to all inbound air cargo shipments. Regular shippers of legitimate cargo should not be adversely affected by the implementation of the PACT Program as air carriers will receive system-generated status updates on their shipment data. 

The system is expected to improve the "messages targeted per hour" rate by approximately ten times. It is also expected to capture 100% of the air carriers that fly to Canada and assess 100% of the data. Currently, only 6% is risk assessed using manual methods and based on a limited number of volunteers.  ", "businessDrivers7": "Transport Canada employs a robust Project Management Framework which ensures business leads articulate the project scope across various project artefacts, which are referred to over the course of the project and altered (as necessary) through change requests as part of the project’s governance. The scope includes a specific dataset that follows a pre-defined industry standard. This is verified as part of an onboarding process to ensure only relevant data is transmitted. The system is restricted to specific users on the Transport Canada intranet and each user’s role is reflected in their permissions. A training program tailors the material to the trainee’s role in the system. ", "businessDrivers9": "item1-0", "businessDrivers10": "The PACT pilot began using Microsoft Outlook and Excel, and with a limited number of volunteers, the maximum capacity of the software was reached. In addition, when we extrapolated on the capacity of our existing staff to determine what we'd need to target all inbound shipments, it was not feasible. Automation is not preferred; it's necessary.", "businessDrivers11": ["item1-3"], "riskProfile5": "item2-0", "impact30": "item2-2", "impact22": "item1-2", "impact24": "item1-1", "privacyImplementation7": "item2-0", "businessDrivers8": "The system was initially configured to realize efficiencies that could not be realized under a manually operated regime. The deployment of a technological solution was the only means to provide a meaningful layer of security for inbound air cargo. Any Pre-Loading Advanced Cargo Information (PLACI) program without such an advanced technological solution would create gross inefficiencies along the supply chain. As such, no trade-offs were considered. ", "decisionSector3": "The decision that is automated is whether to allow shipments to proceed without interference based on a combination of risk indicators. The PACT advanced algorithm will generate an inbound cargo risk score for each shipment based on specific parameters. Based on this score shipments that have pre-defined low-risk characteristics will be automatically assessed; whereas those that fall outside those parameters will be reviewed by a human case-by-case. ", "impact31": "The system will assist PACT analysts in risk assessing shipments by displaying relative risk ratings with relevant information such as whether the shipment follows a known trade pattern. Shipments that have pre-defined low-risk characteristics will be automatically assessed; whereas those that fall outside those parameters will be reviewed by a human case-by-case.", "impact25": "A Privacy Impact Assessment is underway and all safeguards are being put in place to ensure that any personal information that is collected is stored and disposed of in keeping with all applicable laws in Canada. As the personal information of shippers collected by the program is limited to the shipper or consignor’s name, address, and the commodity description, there are few if any anticipated effects on the equality or dignity of individuals. Additionally, the use of advanced analytics as part of the risk assessment will significantly reduce the potential for unintentional bias that could otherwise impact individuals’ dignity and equality rights. Finally, the decision that is automated by the PACT algorithmic system will have little to no impact on individuals’ autonomy because the function of the system is limited to the calculation of the risk of a shipment, and not to its delay or denial of loading. Any decision to prevent a shipment from being transported by air that may follow from that calculation of risk (and that could be construed to limit individuals’ autonomy in opting to use this mode of shipping), remains under the purview of human analysts who will take into consideration a multitude of factors each time a shipment is presented. As the risk factors are fluid (e.g. a trade pattern fluctuates over time), each decision is unique. ", "aboutDataSource13": "The input data is made up of 8 data elements found on airwaybills: shipper and consignee names and addresses, commodity description, weight, piece count, and airwaybill number. This information is shared with Transport Canada by air carriers through a web API.", "privacyImplementation5": "The PACT Program’s Privacy Impact Assessment will review the collection, use, disclosure, and retention of personal information collected as PLACI under the Canadian Aviation Security Regulations, 2012 (CASR 2012). Assessments of the privacy and security safeguards applied to the data repositories that contain the collected PLACI, and of the role and access controls of TC staff executing the PACT Program are also within the scope of the PIA. 

Through amendments to the CASR 2012, air carriers are required to submit a specific dataset for all inbound air cargo in support of air cargo security. Automating part of PACT’s processes allows for the collection, sorting and risk assessment of 100% of the submitted data. This rate of risk assessment was not deemed feasible without automation. 

The draft PIA is being reviewed by Transport Canada's ATIP division, and the timeline for its completion aligns with the coming into force of the regulations in fall 2024. ", "impact20": "Air carriers submit a mandatory dataset to Transport Canada (shipper and consignee name and address, cargo weight, description and piece count, and air waybill number) and each field is checked for data accuracy (e.g. expected character length, character type, etc.). Specifically, natural language processing and fuzzy matching are used to interpret free text fields in the air waybill to clarify and group the commodity descriptions into meaningful categories (e.g. jeans/denim/pants = clothes). The system then applies an automated risk algorithm that calculates a score. Shipments that fall above a risk threshold are presented for the user for further risk analysis. Targeters review the results and use subject matter expertise to clear the shipment or apply a risk mitigation.", "impact21": "Outputs of the system are: an inbound cargo risk score displayed to PACT analysts for each shipment, and electronic messages sent to the air carrier.  

The inbound cargo risk score delineates those shipments that require further review from those that do not. 

The electronic messages let the air carrier know if the data is complete, assessed, or flagged for an RFI, RFS, or a DNL notice. There are two possible automated messages sent by the system when shipment data is received: error codes are sent automatically when incomplete data is received by the system, and assessment complete messages are sent automatically for low-risk shipments once they have been processed by the advanced algorithm. Status messages are sent once a human targeter has made a determination on shipments that fall above the low-risk threshold. These status messages can confirm that the assessment is complete or indicate the need for the air carrier to implement an identified mitigation action.", "impact23": "The system will receive, sort and apply an algorithm to a volume of data that the team was not able to process using available tools such as Microsoft Excel.", "impact14": "A shipper whose shipment is re-screened or prevented from being transported on the flight could incur additional costs. When a shipper opts to ship via air cargo, they agree to terms and conditions that apply to its use, including any applicable fees."}
